verses.commentary: If anyone could be justified by works, Abraham could. His example will prove justification by faith.

If Abraham was justified by works, he had something to boast about, but not before God. Human boasting might exist among men, but not in God's presence. No one can boast before God because all stand guilty.
Credited (logizomai, λογίζομαι) is an accounting term: righteousness is reckoned, imputed, counted to Abraham's account. Faith, not works, was the ground of his justification.
To the one who works, wages are not credited as a gift but as an obligation. Works create debt; the employer owes the worker. This is the principle of merit. But salvation is not by merit.
Not the godly but the ungodly. Faith, not works, is credited as righteousness. Salvation is by trust, not effort.
Psalm 32 describes the blessing of forgiveness apart from works.
Forgiveness is a covering of sin, not the absence of sin. God deals with sin by covering it through atonement.
Non-imputation of sin: God does not charge sin to the believer's account. This is the negative side of justification (sin not counted). The positive side is righteousness credited.
Abraham's example will answer this.
Abraham was justified in Genesis 15, before he was circumcised in Genesis 17. Faith preceded the covenant sign.

He received circumcision as a sign, a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while still uncircumcised. Circumcision confirmed what already existed. It was a sign (pointing to reality) and a seal (authenticating reality), but not the cause of righteousness. So then, he is the father of all who believe but have not been circumcised, in order that righteousness might be credited to them. Abraham is the father of believing Gentiles. Faith, not circumcision, makes one Abraham's child.
Circumcision without faith does not make one Abraham's child.
Law-keeping was not the condition of the promise; faith was.
Law and promise are incompatible as bases of inheritance. Promise implies grace; law implies works.
Transgression (parabasis, παράβασις) is breaking a known command. Law makes sin explicit and culpable. Law produces wrath, not salvation.
Grace is the ground; faith is the means. This guarantees the promise to all who believe, Jew and Gentile. Works would limit the promise; faith opens it to all.

As it is written: I have made you a father of many nations (Genesis 17:5). Abraham is the father in the sight of God, in whom he believed, the God who gives life to the dead and calls into being things that were not. God's creative power is the basis of Abraham's faith. God creates life from death (Isaac from a dead womb) and calls things into existence by His word.
Abraham's faith was against human hope but grounded in divine promise. He believed God's word despite impossible circumstances.
Abraham did not deny reality; he faced it. Yet his faith did not weaken. True faith acknowledges difficulty but trusts God's power.
Faith glorifies God by trusting His word above circumstances.
Full persuasion (plērophoreō, πληροφορέω) means complete conviction. Abraham's faith was in God's ability and integrity.
This is the climax: Abraham's faith, not his works, justified him.
digmatic. His experience establishes the pattern for all believers.
We are justified the same way Abraham was: by faith. The object of faith is different (Jesus, not Isaac), but the principle is the same. God's power to raise the dead is the ground of our faith.
His resurrection secured justification (vindication). Both are necessary. Death pays the penalty; resurrection declares acceptance. Our justification is grounded in Christ's finished work.
